title: A tradition worth keeping in a small town
summary: Describe one local tradition in a small town and defend why it should survive.
topic: A tradition worth keeping in a small town
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a tradition in a small town (anywhere in the world) and argue why it should be kept. Be concrete: when it happens, who runs it, what it costs. Use modals for argument, relative clauses, and at least one linker of concession (although / even though).
hintStarters: What would be lost if it stopped is…

hintVocab.meaningVi: giữ cho điều gì đó sống động

sampleText:
Every year in mid-August, the small town of Óbidos, in central Portugal, closes its main street for one Friday evening and turns it into a long communal dinner. Tables are set up end to end, lanterns are strung across the cobblestones, and anyone who lives in the town, or who happens to be passing through, is welcome to eat. There is a small fee for the meal, which goes to the local fire brigade. I have been there twice, and I'd argue this tradition should be kept exactly as it is, even though it would be easy to ruin.

What makes it work is that the town does it themselves. The cooks are not a catering company; they are three older women who have been making the same stews since the 1980s, helped by teenagers who would otherwise be on their phones. The mayor, who I have never seen wear a tie, carries plates. A retired carpenter tunes the speakers. Although the food is plain — bean stew, grilled sardines, bread, watermelon — it tastes better than it should, because everyone around you knows where it came from. An outsider can sit down at any table. People shift along the bench, ask where you are from, refill your glass, and then go back to their own conversations. Nobody performs hospitality; they just do it.

Some people will say that this kind of dinner could become a tourist attraction, with tickets, branded napkins, and a slot on a food channel. They might be right that it would make money. However, the moment you sell the experience, the experience changes. The teenagers would be replaced by serving staff in uniform. The fire brigade would receive less, the catering company more. The mayor would stop carrying plates, because plates would be carried for him. What would be lost is the strange, almost embarrassing pride a small town feels when it cooks for strangers without expecting praise. If the dinner survives in its current, slightly chaotic form for another twenty years, Óbidos will still be a town. If it is sold, it will become a brochure of a town, and that is not the same thing at all.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: giăng ngang trên đường lát đá

sampleAnnotations.phrase: would otherwise be on their phones
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: nếu không thì sẽ dán mắt vào điện thoại
niềm tự hào gần như ngượng ngùng
một tờ rơi quảng cáo về thị trấn

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Linker 'even though' for concession
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: '…even though it would be easy to ruin' dùng 'even though' để thừa nhận một khả năng tiêu cực mà không từ bỏ luận điểm chính.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: '…even though it would be easy to ruin' uses 'even though' to concede a possible negative while keeping the main argument.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modal 'would' for hypothetical consequence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The teenagers would be replaced by serving staff in uniform' dùng 'would' để mô tả hậu quả giả định nếu tình huống thay đổi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The teenagers would be replaced by serving staff in uniform' uses 'would' to describe a hypothetical consequence if the situation changed.

sampleTranslationVi:
Mỗi năm vào giữa tháng Tám, thị trấn nhỏ Óbidos ở miền trung Bồ Đào Nha đóng đường chính trong một tối thứ Sáu và biến nó thành một bữa tối chung dài. Bàn được kê nối đuôi nhau, đèn lồng giăng ngang trên đường lát đá, và bất cứ ai sống trong thị trấn, hoặc tình cờ đi ngang qua, đều được chào đón đến ăn. Có một khoản phí nhỏ cho bữa ăn, sẽ chuyển cho đội cứu hỏa địa phương. Tôi đã đến đó hai lần, và tôi cho rằng truyền thống này nên được giữ đúng như nó đang là, dù sẽ rất dễ bị phá hỏng.

Điều khiến nó hiệu quả là vì cả thị trấn tự làm. Đầu bếp không phải công ty cung cấp suất ăn; họ là ba người phụ nữ lớn tuổi đã nấu cùng những món hầm đó từ những năm 1980, có sự giúp đỡ của những thiếu niên mà nếu không thì sẽ dán mắt vào điện thoại. Ông thị trưởng, người tôi chưa bao giờ thấy đeo cà vạt, bưng đĩa. Một người thợ mộc về hưu chỉnh loa. Dù món ăn đơn giản — súp đậu hầm, cá mòi nướng, bánh mì, dưa hấu — nó ngon hơn lẽ ra phải vậy, vì mọi người xung quanh bạn đều biết nó đến từ đâu. Người ngoài có thể ngồi xuống ở bất kỳ bàn nào. Người ta dịch dọc băng ghế, hỏi bạn đến từ đâu, rót thêm rượu cho bạn, rồi quay lại với cuộc trò chuyện của mình. Không ai trình diễn lòng hiếu khách; họ chỉ làm việc đó.

Một số người sẽ nói rằng kiểu bữa tối này có thể trở thành một điểm thu hút du lịch, với vé, khăn ăn có thương hiệu, và một suất phát trên kênh ẩm thực. Họ có thể đúng rằng nó sẽ kiếm được tiền. Tuy nhiên, khoảnh khắc bạn bán trải nghiệm, trải nghiệm sẽ thay đổi. Những thiếu niên sẽ bị thay thế bởi nhân viên phục vụ mặc đồng phục. Đội cứu hỏa sẽ nhận được ít hơn, công ty cung cấp suất ăn nhận nhiều hơn. Ông thị trưởng sẽ ngừng bưng đĩa, vì đĩa sẽ được bưng cho ông. Điều bị mất sẽ là niềm tự hào kỳ lạ, gần như ngượng ngùng mà một thị trấn nhỏ cảm nhận được khi nấu cho người lạ mà không mong được khen. Nếu bữa tối còn sống ở hình thức hiện tại, hơi hỗn loạn của nó thêm hai mươi năm nữa, Óbidos vẫn sẽ là một thị trấn. Nếu nó bị bán, nó sẽ trở thành một tờ rơi quảng cáo về thị trấn, và đó không phải là cùng một thứ.

options: I'd argue this tradition should be kept, even though it would be easy to ruin.
I'd argue this tradition should be kept, even though to ruin would be easy.
I'd argue this tradition should be kept, even thought it would be easy to ruin.
I'd argue this tradition should be kept, even though it will be easy ruin.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even though' introduces a contrasting clause; the rest of the sentence uses 'would' for a hypothetical.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even though' + clause; second clause uses 'would + base verb'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the natural use of modal 'would' for hypothetical consequence.
options: The teenagers would replaced by serving staff in uniform.
The teenagers would be replaced by serving staff in uniform.
The teenagers would been replace by serving staff in uniform.
The teenagers will be replaced by serving staff in uniform.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would + be + past participle' is the passive form of a hypothetical consequence.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause correctly?
options: The mayor, which I have never seen wear a tie, carries plates.
The mayor, who I have never seen wear a tie, carries plates.
The mayor, what I have never seen wear a tie, carries plates.
The mayor, that I have never seen him wear a tie, carries plates.
mcQuestions.explanation: Use 'who' for people; do not repeat the subject inside the relative clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option has the correct present perfect for repeated experience?
options: I have been there twice.
I am being there twice.
I have went there twice.
I been there twice.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have been + place' is the standard present perfect for travel experience.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Have been to' or 'have been there' for travel.

options: Although the food is plain, it tastes better than it should.
Although the food plain is, it tastes better than should it.
Although of the food is plain, it tastes better than it should.
Although the food is plain so it tastes better than it should.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' is the standard pattern; no 'so', no 'of'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option has the correct preposition?
options: Tables are set up end to end.
Tables are set up end on end.
Tables are set up end with end.
Tables are set up end by end.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'End to end' is the fixed phrase for things lined up touching.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'might' correctly as a hedged modal?
options: They might right that it would make money.
They might be right that it would make money.
They might to be right that it would make money.
They might been right that it would make money.
mcQuestions.explanation: Modal verbs are followed by the bare infinitive ('be').

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a 'would otherwise' construction naturally?
options: Helped by teenagers who would otherwise be on their phones.
Helped by teenagers who otherwise will be on their phones.
Helped by teenagers who would on their phones otherwise be.
Helped by teenagers who would otherwise being on their phones.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would otherwise + base verb' expresses the alternative situation if the current one didn't exist.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence has correct article use?
options: The mayor would stop carrying plates, because plates would be carried for him.
Mayor would stop carrying the plates, because the plates would be carried for him.
The mayor would stop carrying a plates, because a plates would be carried for him.
A mayor would stop carrying plates, because the plates would be carried for him.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The mayor' is a specific person; 'plates' as a general activity needs no article.
mcQuestions.hint: Specific person → 'the'; generic activity → no article.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option is the most natural way to express a hypothetical loss?
options: What would be lost is the strange, almost embarrassing pride a small town feels.
What is lost will be the strange, almost embarrassing pride a small town feels.
What lost would the strange, almost embarrassing pride a small town feels.
What would lost is the strange, almost embarrassing pride a small town feels.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What would be lost' is a cleft hypothetical using 'would + be + past participle'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a tradition in your own town or family that quietly survives because nobody has tried to professionalise it? What would change if someone did?
